C.direct_quant_exec: YES (mmap, per-row tile dequant, no dense shadow)
C.f2_120b: bounded bridge (fail-closed on absent asset + synthetic MoE proof)
C.name: Event Horizon engine (direct-quant + Metal)
C.subbit_direct: YES (0.401 BPW ternary latent factor, executed directly)
C.throughput: ~3-6 tok/s direct-quant CPU (Metal 15x on the bottleneck; generate() uses CPU for bit-identical parity)

honest_caveats: The 120B F2 bridge could NOT run against the real GPT-OSS-120B: that asset is absent on this machine, so the real path fails closed and the MoE contract is proven on a bounded SYNTHETIC parent-shaped fixture. No 120B capability is claimed.

C's CPU decode throughput (~3-6 tok/s) is LOWER than B's (9.68) because C dequantizes on the fly (incl. per-element f16 rounding across the 49k-row LM head) rather than precomputing dense f32. This is the honest cost of removing the dense shadow; the Metal path (15x on the bottleneck) is the intended remedy and is measured but not yet wired into the decode loop.
Metal logits are within 6.7e-6 of CPU (argmax agrees) but not bit-identical; parity is therefore taken on the CPU path.

features_to_import_into_final_seed: From B: an OPTIONAL dequant-at-load 'dense-fast' profile could be offered for tiny models where RAM is abundant and raw CPU throughput matters - but it reintroduces the dense shadow, so the preferred path is to SIMD/Metal the direct-quant gemv instead.

Wire the validated Metal LM-head into the decode loop (with a near-tie guard) so C gets both bounded memory AND Metal speed while preserving bit-identical tokens - the single highest-value optimization.
The shared authority (record/state/gravity/pack/evidence, ~420 LOC) is identical across B and C; the final Seed keeps it as-is.
selection.not_selected_disposition: A (PR #28) and B (PR #29) are kept as SEALED EXPERIMENTS (branches + tags retained); their draft PRs are superseded by C. Not merged.
selection.rationale: Selection is NOT by LOC alone. C dominates on the axes that matter for Hawking's mission (sub-bit-first, giant parents, hardware-native): direct-quant execution with 3.5x lower memory than B (212 vs 726 MB), Metal-native acceleration (15x measured on the bottleneck), genuine sub-bit direct execution (<1.0 BPW), and an MoE-ready IR + 120B F2 bridge that make it giant-model-extensible - all in 2,204 LOC (under the 3,000 gravitational target) with the SAME bit-identical parity and in-crate auditability as B. A is rejected: it delegates model math to the 52k predecessor engine (the very thing the Seed exists to escape) and ships ~53k LOC. B is a superb pure-dense-CPU result but its f32 dequant-at-load (726 MB, no Metal, no direct-quant, no sub-bit, no MoE path) does not scale to giant parents - the dense shadow is exactly what C removes.
